In the new client application used specifically for the MFA request of the Toolbox on the Train system, the following parameters were set to true: EnableJWT RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders EnableJWT activates the issuance of JSON Web Tokens in the authentication process. If enabled, ADFS issues JWTs instead of other token formats such as SAML. These tokens are evaluated by the target application. If the target system does not support JWTs or cannot validate their signature, authentication fails. RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders forwards MFA requirements to configured claims providers. This delegates MFA execution to external providers and increases complexity in authentication flow, logging, and auditing. The combination of these parameters can lead to login failures if the target system is not designed to handle JWTs or external MFA claims. We have documented the configuration to avoid future issues. From our perspective, no further problems are expected after adjusting the parameters. If MFA works stably and the Train environment is reachable again, the login topic can be considered resolved. Kind regards Employee 21 Notes: 26.11.24 Ticket opened by Employee 16. 26.11.24 Ticket assigned REPORT 2: # TRAIN CE / ADFS Integration: CE Train Server, ADFS Client Registration, and Toolbox MFA This community centers on the TRAIN CE/CE Train server integration that relies on on‑premises ADFS for authentication. A newly registered ADFS client used by the Toolbox to orchestrate MFA had EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders enabled in PROD, which caused ADFS to issue JWTs and to forward MFA to external claims providers; those changes broke non‑browser integrations and produced widespread login/connectivity failures across environments. The incident affected the Train CE server, involved remediation actions (repair installation, federation metadata updates, certificate removals), and required coordinated troubleshooting by internal support engineers, the Identity/Architecture team and an external vendor (Avanade). Secondary factors observed during remediation included stale device compliance from hybrid Azure AD‑joined devices and Conditional Access evaluations that contributed to access variability for some users. The investigation produced both an ADFS configuration documentation artifact and guidance in a broader Identity & Authentication blueprint to prevent recurrence and to inform escalation and monitoring practices. ## Root cause: misconfigured ADFS client registration (EnableJWT + RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders) on a new client The investigation identified a new client application whose ADFS client registration had EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders set to true; this caused ADFS to issue JWTs and to delegate MFA to external claims providers, which the downstream Toolbox/Train integration did not expect and therefore failed to authenticate. The new client registration and the parameter choices are explicitly recorded as the root cause and were adjusted to match the TEST environment to resolve the failures [Data: Entities (249,248,240,241); Relationships (390,370)]. Recommendation: enforce a documented client‑registration review process that verifies token format and MFA delegation flags before promoting client registrations to PROD, and add a checklist item to the CI/CD or change request to confirm EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders settings for non‑browser integrations. ## Production impact and remediation steps applied to the CE Train server The CE Train server (TRAIN CE) experienced connectivity and authentication failures across users and environments; remediation steps recorded include a repair installation of the CE Train server (02.12.24), federation metadata updates on ADFS, and removal/rotation of old federation certificates—followed by ADFS parameter changes that restored correct behaviour. These actions and the target system are captured in the incident notes and ticket timeline [Data: Entities (252,235,254,253); Relationships (405,409,410,367)]. Recommendation: codify the validated remediation sequence (repair, federation metadata verification, certificate refresh, client parameter alignment) into a runbook for TRAIN CE integrations so responders can execute a repeatable playbook during future authentication incidents. ## Stakeholder and escalation map: support engineers, identity/architecture, application owners and external vendor involvement Multiple named stakeholders participated in triage and remediation: Employee 21 acted as lead analyst/owner for related incidents and executed configuration changes and device sync operations; Employee 14 reported and coordinated the initial ticket; Employee 24 and Admin appear in the ticket transfer chain; Avanade participated as an external troubleshooting partner; and the affected tenant and application owners are Customer Company 11AG / 11CE TRAIN. The ticket and assignment flows show cross‑team responsibility and vendor engagement during the incident [Data: Entities (232,4,231,10,229,230); Relationships (353,361,391,17,365)]. Recommendation: publish a concise stakeholder contact matrix and an escalation path (first responder/System Engineer -> Identity/Architecture -> Application Owner -> Vendor) and include roles, expected SLAs, and preferred communication channels for TRAIN CE‑class authentication incidents. ## Conditional Access and device sync were contributing factors for some access failures but were not the primary root cause Conditional Access was evaluated and did not present an outright blocking rule for the affected sign‑ins; however, some hybrid Azure AD‑joined devices reported stale or 'unknown' compliance for 6–8 hours, causing the 'Require compliant device' CA policy to evaluate as not satisfied and producing access variability for certain users. Employee 21 triggered manual device synchronization as part of remediation to update device compliance state faster [Data: Entities (683,680,688,684); Relationships (1199,1201,1206,1189)]. Recommendation: implement monitoring/alerts for device‑sync latency and include device synchronization health in incident triage dashboards; where appropriate, create temporary CA policy exceptions documented in change control but tied to a limited remediation window. ## Logging, monitoring and documentation gaps identified and addressed with configuration documentation and blueprint guidance The post‑incident artifacts include an ADFS configuration documentation entry capturing the corrected client registration settings and the investigation details, authored/confirmed by Employee 21, and a separate Identity & Authentication blueprint that prescribes decision trees, token format guidance, and escalation routes. The blueprint highlights the need for correlated logging across ADFS, claims providers and Entra ID to diagnose delegated MFA and JWT/SAML token flows [Data: Entities (244,232,207,219,208); Relationships (364,317,330)]. Recommendation: ensure the ADFS configuration documentation is versioned in the configuration management repository and update monitoring/alerting to collect token issuance events, claims‑provider logs and correlation IDs to shorten future diagnosis time. ## Token format and non‑browser flow mismatch risk (JWT vs SAML) for applications Enabling JWT issuance via EnableJWT changes the token format emitted by ADFS (JWT/OIDC) and requires that consuming applications support and validate JWTs; the blueprint warns that non‑browser integrations and service accounts commonly fail when token formats change unexpectedly. The incident demonstrates this risk where the new client caused JWT issuance that the Train integration could not process [Data: Entities (240,242,215,216); Relationships (379,324,334)]. Recommendation: require application owners to declare supported token formats in application inventories and gate any client registration change that switches token formats with explicit owner approval and a test plan validating non‑browser flows. ## Process and governance weakness: PROD‑only client registration change and insufficient pre‑deployment verification Investigators initially considered and briefly excluded ADFS because only PROD had been modified over the weekend; the error reproduced across environments, and only later was the misconfigured new client identified as the root cause—indicating a gap in change control or rollout validation for client registrations in PROD. The ticket timeline and decision notes record that the misconfigured registration was corrected to match TEST [Data: Entities (21,249,251); Relationships (65,228,390)]. Recommendation: implement formal change controls for ADFS client registrations (including peer review and a rollback plan), require a documented mapping between TEST and PROD registration parameters, and add pre‑promotion automated checks that detect risky flags like EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders in non‑browser client contexts. ## Operationalizing the Decision Tree and runbook: where to escalate and what telemetry to collect The Identity & Authentication blueprint includes a decision tree for authentication issues (question flow for browser vs non‑browser, token type checks, MFA delegation checks and escalation criteria). The incident suggests operationalizing that decision tree into an incident runbook covering when to escalate to the Identity/Architecture team, what logs to collect (ADFS token issuance, claims provider events, Entra ID sign‑in and sync logs), and what immediate mitigations to try (client parameter rollback, device sync, certificate checks) [Data: Entities (225,207,220,219); Relationships (322,348,330)]. Recommendation: convert the blueprint decision tree into a one‑page runbook with command snippets for log collection, prioritized diagnostics, and a checklist for safe client parameter changes to shorten mean‑time‑to‑resolution and reduce handoff friction.

answer: . - Intermittent external guest access to a SharePoint folder (works then “access denied”; re‑adding helps only temporarily) - Prior cases show the common root cause is a cloud vs on‑prem group/object collision (Azure AD Connect / provisioning): a cloud‑native group containing guest accounts was replaced by/superseded by a synchronized on‑prem group object with the same displayName but different objectId, so guests were no longer treated as members by services that evaluate the on‑prem object. Engineers used Microsoft Graph / Entra exports, compared objectIds and performed group consolidation + a full Azure AD Connect sync to fix it [Documented]

Due to synchronization, effective group membership for external users was not correctly applied. The group structure was consolidated and fully synchronized. Access is now stable. Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 64: # Azure AD Connect vs Cloud COMPLIANCE_TEAM — External Guest Access Loss (SharePoint) This community centers on a cloud-native COMPLIANCE_TEAM group and an On‑Prem COMPLIANCE_TEAM group that share a display name but have distinct ObjectIDs. Azure AD Connect synchronized the On‑Prem group into Entra ID/Azure AD, which progressively replaced or caused SharePoint to evaluate the synchronized On‑Prem object instead of the cloud-native group. Because external guest accounts lived only in the cloud-native group (they were not present On‑Prem), those external guests lost SharePoint access while internal users (present in the On‑Prem group) retained access. A system engineer reproduced the issue, used Microsoft Graph and the Entra ID portal to compare group membership and source attributes, and guided remediation steps including consolidation/cleanup and a full sync to restore intended access mappings. [Data: Entities (265,266,267,268,269,270); Relationships (437,440,441,445,446,435,430)] ## Root cause: Azure AD Connect synchronization caused the cloud-native group to be overwritten/evaluated as the On‑Prem synchronized object, removing external guests from effective membership Investigators observed that Azure AD Connect gradually overwrote or deactivated the cloud-native COMPLIANCE_TEAM group with the synchronized On‑Prem group (same display name, different Object IDs), which changed the effective membership evaluated by SharePoint and resulted in external guests losing access. This behavior is documented in the service description for Azure AD Connect and the relationship between the cloud and On‑Prem group objects. [Data: Entities (269,265,266); Relationships (437,440,446)] The critical aspect is the display-name collision combined with synchronization semantics: SharePoint was referencing the synchronized On‑Prem object (the object with the On‑Prem ObjectID), not the separate cloud-native group that contained guest members, so membership evaluation excluded cloud-only guest accounts. This mismatch between object identity and display name is the immediate mechanism of access loss. [Data: Entities (265,266,267); Relationships (437,444,435)] ## Scope and impact: External guests lost access while internal users remained unaffected External project members provisioned as Guest accounts in Entra ID/Azure AD lost SharePoint access because they were members of the cloud-native COMPLIANCE_TEAM, which was distinct from the synchronized On‑Prem group used by SharePoint. Internal users were members of the On‑Prem COMPLIANCE_TEAM and therefore continued to have access. This differential outcome is captured by the entity relationships showing guest membership in the cloud group and internal membership in the On‑Prem group. [Data: Entities (267,268,265,266); Relationships (437,441)] Investigators also noted that affected accounts had differing "source" attributes visible in the Entra ID portal; examining the source attribute helped correlate which accounts originated On‑Prem versus in the cloud, which was instrumental in identifying why some users were impacted and others were not. This observation ties the access symptoms back to identity source metadata in Entra ID. [Data: Entities (270,267); Relationships (445)] ## Investigation and diagnostics used: System Engineer 14, Microsoft Graph, and Entra ID inspection A system engineer conducted in-depth analysis: reviewing Azure AD Connect behavior, inspecting group membership via Microsoft Graph, reproducing the issue in test, and guiding remediation (consolidation, cleanup, full sync). The engineer used Microsoft Graph queries to enumerate members of both the cloud-native and synchronized On‑Prem groups to compare differences in member lists. These investigative actions are documented in the entity and relationship records. [Data: Entities (261,271,269); Relationships (430,431)] The Entra ID portal was used to observe account "source" attributes (e.g., "source = Windows Server AD" vs "source = Azure AD"), providing direct evidence to distinguish which accounts were cloud-native guests and which were synchronized On‑Prem accounts. This combination of Graph API and portal inspection provided the necessary diagnostics for root-cause confirmation. [Data: Entities (270,267); Relationships (445,431)] ## Service dependency: SharePoint permission construct (SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN) referenced the COMPLIANCE_TEAM group, creating an access dependency SharePoint's permission construct SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN was found to contain or reference the COMPLIANCE_TEAM group; investigators examined membership mappings between these constructs during the analysis. Because SharePoint evaluated the group object that Azure AD Connect synchronized, the SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN -> COMPLIANCE_TEAM linkage is the downstream dependency that surfaced the access failure for external guests. [Data: Entities (264,265,266); Relationships (435)] This highlights that access control in SharePoint depended on the directory object identity (ObjectID) rather than purely on display name. Any change in which directory object is presented to SharePoint (for example, due to synchronization overwrite) will directly affect access evaluation for resources protected by SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN. [Data: Relationships (435,446)] ## Identity object collision risk: same display name, different ObjectIDs created a deterministic failure mode There were two distinct COMPLIANCE_TEAM objects: a cloud-native Azure AD group and an On‑Prem AD group synchronized by Azure AD Connect. Both used the same display name but had different ObjectIDs; when the synchronized On‑Prem object replaced or was used by downstream services, cloud-only members were excluded. This naming/collision pattern created a reproducible failure mode tied directly to synchronization behavior. [Data: Entities (265,266); Relationships (444,437,440)] Because Azure AD Connect will surface synchronized objects into Entra ID and object identity is the authoritative binding for resource access, organizations must treat display-name collisions between cloud-native and On‑Prem groups as high risk; this incident demonstrates how that risk materializes as access loss for cloud-only accounts. [Data: Relationships (446,437)] ## Remediation performed: consolidation, cleanup, and a full sync guided by the system engineer restored intended mappings The system engineer guided remediation actions including consolidation and cleanup of the conflicting group objects and performed a full sync to restore the correct group membership and access behavior. The ticket notes that a full synchronization was performed as part of remediation and that the engineer reproduced the issue in test to validate the fix. These documented steps indicate the operational path taken to remediate the outage. [Data: Entities (261,269); Relationships (430)] While the immediate remediation restored access, the records do not include a formal postmortem or a permanent governance control to prevent recurrence; the cleanup and full sync were corrective but the underlying naming/ownership risk remains unless addressed by policy. The available data supports the remediation actions but does not yet show a persistent mitigation mechanism. [Data: Entities (269,261); Relationships (430,437)] ## Governance and monitoring gaps: lack of explicit evidence of group ownership, naming policies, or sync exclusion rules The corpus documents the synchronization collision, diagnostic steps, and corrective actions, but it contains no explicit records describing group ownership assignments, naming conventions to prevent display-name collisions, or Azure AD Connect exclusion/filtering rules applied to groups to prevent cloud-native objects from being overwritten. The absence of these governance artifacts in the provided records indicates potential control gaps that allowed the condition to occur. [Data: Entities (265,266,269); Relationships (437,446,435)] Because SharePoint permission constructs referenced the group object identity and Azure AD Connect can alter which object is authoritative in the cloud, formal controls (group ownership, naming conventions, synchronization scoping/exclusions, and monitoring/alerting for object conflicts) are required to reduce recurrence risk. The evidence of an operational incident plus lack of documented governance in the dataset supports elevating this as a priority for follow-up. [Data: Relationships (435,437,446)]

context: SOURCE 35: ﻿ Ticket summary: Ticket name: KF11: Intune NDES Connector lost communication Ticket number: TI-722423 Company: Customer Company 11 Contact person: Contact Person 8 Owner: Employee 11 Origin: Phone Type: Incident Created on: 27.03.24 12:04 Closure Closure date: 14.06.24 16:10 Closure text: Incident successfully resolved Activities Emails: 27.03.24 13:10 | TI-722423 | KF11: Intune NDES Connector lost communication | Employee 11 | Contact Person 8 Hello Contact Person 8, hello Contact Person 2, Together with the network team, I adjusted the firewall rules and then reinstalled the connector – this time without proxy configuration. After the reinstallation, the connector was displayed as “green” again in Azure (this step is required so that it can properly communicate with the Intune service). Since we had identified login issues, we temporarily excluded the service account from the MFA requirement. After completing the installation, I removed the “Intune Admin” role from the service account again. If we need to reinstall the connector, this role must be temporarily reassigned. During the installation, I followed the Microsoft guide “Configure infrastructure to support SCEP certificate profiles with Microsoft Intune.” Attached you will find logs to quickly verify whether the connector is operating correctly. See blueprint_1. Please do not make any changes to the settings without prior coordination. If issues occur during the Intune deployment, you may contact the Helpdesk or the WPS team at any time. REPORT 24: # Intune NDES Connector — Intune / On-prem NDES / SCEP Certificate Flow This community centers on the Intune NDES Connector, the on-prem connector that bridges Microsoft Intune and the on-premises NDES (Network Device Enrollment Service) to deliver SCEP certificate profiles to Intune-managed devices. The connector's communication failure was resolved by reinstalling the connector without proxy configuration and adjusting firewall rules; Azure then reported the connector as healthy. Operational logs and an internal blueprint were used to validate the connector's restored operation. Key relationships in this community tie the connector to Azure monitoring, proxy configuration, firewall rules, on-prem NDES, SCEP certificate profiles, and Intune itself. ## Intune NDES Connector is the central operational dependency for SCEP certificate issuance The Intune NDES Connector acts as the bridge between Microsoft Intune and the on-premises NDES service and is required to complete SCEP certificate enrollment flows for Intune-managed devices. This central role is shown by its direct relationships to SCEP certificate profiles, the on-prem NDES instance, and the Intune service. Disruption of this connector therefore directly affects certificate issuance and device enrollment workflows [Data: Entities (641, 650, 362), Relationships (1114, 1113, 1116)]. ## Proxy configuration and firewall rules were implicated in the communication failure Evidence in the record indicates the connector was reinstalled without proxy configuration and that adjusting firewall rules allowed the connector to re-establish communication with Intune. Those two configuration areas were both modified during remediation, identifying network/proxy controls as primary suspects in the incident chain. The connector returning to a healthy state in Azure occurred after these changes, linking network controls to the service recovery [Data: Entities (641, 644), Relationships (1109, 1108, 1110)]. ## Service recovery validation used Azure health, operational logs, and an internal blueprint After remediation, Azure was used to observe the connector's health (reported as healthy), and operational logs were attached to validate correct operation. The internal blueprint was referenced alongside logs as a verification resource. context: REPORT 15: # Basel Print Infrastructure — Print Server, MFP-BASEL-01 & Printer VLAN The community centers on centralized Windows print servers that host location-based printer queues and enforce queue-level permissions via Active Directory groups and Group Policy; physical printers (including MFP-BASEL-01) are deployed into a dedicated PRINTER VLAN that requires inter-VLAN routing and firewall/ACL rules so the print server and client workstations can reach them. An operational incident (TI-1622483) combined a misconfigured explicit Deny ACE on the MFP-BASEL-01 queue with recent network segmentation that initially prevented the print server from reaching the printer until routing/firewall rules were corrected; remediation removed the explicit Deny, corrected AD/GPO configuration and adjusted network rules according to the Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard and Decision Tree guidance. Key artifacts that govern behavior and troubleshooting are the Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard and an embedded Decision Tree which prescribe AD-group-based provisioning, GPO-based default printers, SMTP relay restrictions for printer VLANs, and a diagnostic checklist covering spooler, queue permissions and VLAN reachability. [Data: Entities (70,52,137,53,427); Relationships (112,119,704,222,695)] ## Canonical architecture: central Windows print servers + VLAN‑isolated printers, mapped by AD/GPO Printing is implemented as a centralized service: Windows print servers host queues and standardized drivers, and queues are mapped to users via AD groups and Group Policy (location-based queues and defaults). This is the foundational design documented in the Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard and implemented via GPOs and provisioning processes. [Data: Entities (70,718,53,57); Relationships (112,92)] Because printers are placed in a dedicated PRINTER VLAN, reachability depends on inter-VLAN routing and firewall/ACL rules; the standard requires routing so the print server can reach the printers and instructs operators to validate VLAN reachability (ping) as part of diagnostics. This separation centralizes printer topology but creates a dependency between print services and network configuration that must be validated on every VLAN change. [Data: Entities (137,1280); Relationships (715,1312)] ## Incident root cause: overlapping queue-level DENY ACE plus network segmentation The production outage reported in TI-1622483 manifested as an affected user receiving immediate “Access denied” errors while other users' jobs either remained in the queue or disappeared without printing. Troubleshooting identified two correlated root causes: an explicit Deny ACE on the MFP-BASEL-01 queue that overrode group-based Allow permissions, and the printer’s move into a separate PRINTER VLAN that initially prevented the print server from reaching the device until routing/firewall rules were adjusted. Both issues were necessary to explain the mixed symptoms observed. [Data: Entities (73,52,71,137); Relationships (119,704)] This demonstrates a classic dual-failure mode where identity/ACL misconfiguration blocks a single principal while network segmentation blocks server-to-device delivery for other paths — both must be checked in parallel when jobs appear then disappear. [Data: Relationships (704,119)] ## Remediation followed standards: Deny removed, AD/GPO corrected, firewall/routing adjusted Resolution actions documented in the ticket included removal of the explicit Deny ACE on the printer queue, correction of AD group membership and forcing a Group Policy refresh so location-based defaults applied correctly, and adjustment of the firewall/routing rule to allow the print server to reach the PRINTER VLAN. The ticket closure explicitly cites configuration changes implemented according to the Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard. These combined changes restored normal behavior and the customer confirmed functionality. [Data: Entities (427,73,53); Relationships (80,695)] The remediation path aligns to the Decision Tree’s prescribed sequence (permission checks, GPO refresh, VLAN reachability tests) and is therefore repeatable and auditable when executed against the standard. [Data: Entities (164); Relationships (1312,1311)] ## Operational decision-tree and diagnostics are present and were leveraged An operational Decision Tree and Use Cases document exists to drive consistent troubleshooting (checklist of spooler state, queue visibility, AD membership, VLAN reachability, paused queues, and test prints). The decision tree specifically includes steps for VLAN reachability/ping, paused-queue checks, and print‑job presence/absence to route diagnostics. This artifact was used to guide verification and is a key enabler of consistent incident handling. [Data: Entities (164); Relationships (1312,1311,1310)] Maintaining and enforcing use of this decision tree reduces mean time to repair by ensuring both identity/permission and network segments are validated early in the workflow. ## GPO/AD design fragility: overlapping or parallel groups produce incorrect default mappings Group Policy assigns location-based default printers by evaluating AD group membership; overlapping or parallel AD groups have previously caused the Zurich printer to be applied to Basel users until memberships and GPO targeting were corrected. The environment relies on single-purpose AD groups (no parallel groups) per the provisioning rules in the standard, and deviations here directly cause incorrect printer mapping and user experience issues. [Data: Entities (57,64); Relationships (92,88)] Recommendation: enforce strict group-naming and lifecycle controls, validate nested memberships during change windows, and include membership checks in the GPO deployment runbook to prevent cross-location mapping errors. ## SMTP/scan‑to‑mail compliance gap for new PRINTER VLAN The PRINTER VLAN’s IP range had not been added to the organization’s SMTP relay allow list when the VLAN was created, causing external SMTP relay attempts from devices in that VLAN to be denied until the range was whitelisted. The Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard prohibits printers sending directly to the Internet and requires usage of a dedicated SMTP relay whose access list is maintained. This is a non-printing impact that affects scan‑to‑mail functionality and compliance with the standard. [Data: Entities (137,53); Relationships (222)] Network and change-management checklists must include updates to SMTP relay ACLs when introducing new device VLANs to avoid secondary outages impacting scan workflows. ## Monitoring and diagnostics gap: spooler state alone is insufficient — include queue ACL and VLAN checks The Print Spooler service on the server was observed to be active while print jobs were not reaching the printer; restarting the spooler can transiently change symptoms but does not correct queue‑level ACLs or network routing problems. The decision tree explicitly separates spooler health from queue status and VLAN reachability checks, indicating the need for multi-dimensional monitoring and alerting (service health + queue ACL anomalies + inter-VLAN reachability). [Data: Entities (159,429); Relationships (255,1312)] Operationally, alerts that only surface a running spooler but declining print completion rates should automatically trigger queue-ACL and VLAN-reachability checks as part of the incident playbook. ## Clear stakeholder boundaries but ad hoc coordination required: IT Infrastructure, Network Team, site contacts Document ownership and operational responsibilities are defined: IT Infrastructure authors and enforces the Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard and provisions printers; the Network Team is responsible for VLAN configuration and firewall changes and was engaged to adjust the firewall rule that blocked server-to-printer traffic; the ticket lists a site contact who confirmed restoration. However, VLAN and SMTP ACL changes show that coordination between these groups must be formalized in the change process to prevent related failures. [Data: Entities (717,428,427); Relationships (712,222,695)] Recommendation: define explicit escalation and approval paths for changes that affect both identity/provisioning and network segmentation, include cross-team sign-off in the change template, and publish a short runbook for combined print-network incidents. Several mitigation requests (a firewall/geolocation control for non‑MFA sign‑ins and multiple service‑account exclusions) and ad‑hoc changes (temporary MFA exclusions, NDES connector reinstall without proxy) indicate operational shortcuts that increase security and governance exposure while restoring service availability. ## Consulting Sample Company is the centralized service provider and ticketing hub CONSULTING SAMPLE COMPANY acts as the primary incident owner and operator of the customer support portal; it performs end‑to‑end incident handling including triage, configuration changes, conditional access fixes, Azure AD Connect synchronizations, and test validations across multiple customer tenants [Data: Entities (50), Entities (620), Relationships (1060)]. This repeatable pattern provides a usable escalation pathway (consultant engineer → responsible Systems Engineer → network/RAR & RMA/CDOM) but also signals reliance on named individuals and groups rather than explicit, enforced runbooks; where runbook gaps exist, ad‑hoc temporary fixes (e.g., temporary MFA exclusions) were applied to progress remediation [Data: Relationships (466,455), Entities (277)]. ## Credential‑harvesting phishing incident (TI‑858473) created immediate containment and policy change requests TI‑858473 describes a high‑priority phishing event affecting CUSTOMER COMPANY 12 where two PDF attachments contained links to a fake Microsoft login page and credentials were exfiltrated to ALGINA.CO; the PDFs were captured as evidence in the portal and blocked as part of initial containment [Data: Entities (80,98,97,96), Relationships (132,158,157)]. Employee 11 removed and reinstalled agents on KF10AZADS01 and KF10AZADS02, cleared alerts, and monitored the hosts for recurrence; portal notes describe coordination with the Network Team for firewall settings and a temporary service‑account MFA exclusion to permit installation and configuration of the NDES/SCEP connector without proxy [Data: Entities (274,288,284,285,286,277), Relationships (456,472,473,474)]. These remediation steps solved the immediate sensor health failures, but the use of temporary account exclusions and NDES connector reinstall without proxy indicates configuration workarounds that should be reconciled with security baselines and documented in a controlled change to prevent recurring sensor degradation or privileged‑access drift [Data: Entities (277), Relationships (456,472)]. ## Network and DNS changes are implicated in Scan‑to‑Mail and device discovery failures Customer Company 60 AG reported recent network/DNS and external mail security changes (including new DNS forwarding) made before the onset of Scan‑to‑Mail delivery failures and device discovery/routing issues; the customer noted these changes as potentially relevant to the incidents, pointing to cross‑domain dependencies between network/DNS configuration and print/scan mailflow functionality [Data: Entities (47)]. The evidence suggests insufficient automated enforcement or a lack of templated, auditable runbooks to ensure that temporary exceptions are time‑boxed, documented, and reviewed by governance stakeholders [Data: Entities (87,277), Relationships (136,455)]. ## Operational observability is present but could be better tied to runbooks and ownership SLAs Tickets include concrete telemetry and artifacts — print‑queue states, gpupdate actions, Defender agent sensor states, portal PDF attachments, and explicit remediation steps — demonstrating good incident evidence capture which can support runbook creation [Data: Entities (510,691,288,98), Relationships (857,1219,475,132)]. In the new client application used specifically for the MFA request of the Toolbox on the Train system, the following parameters were set to true: EnableJWT RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders EnableJWT activates the issuance of JSON Web Tokens in the authentication process. If enabled, ADFS issues JWTs instead of other token formats such as SAML. These tokens are evaluated by the target application. If the target system does not support JWTs or cannot validate their signature, authentication fails. RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders forwards MFA requirements to configured claims providers. This delegates MFA execution to external providers and increases complexity in authentication flow, logging, and auditing. The combination of these parameters can lead to login failures if the target system is not designed to handle JWTs or external MFA claims. We have documented the configuration to avoid future issues. From our perspective, no further problems are expected after adjusting the parameters. If MFA works stably and the Train environment is reachable again, the login topic can be considered resolved. Kind regards Employee 21 Notes: 26.11.24 Ticket opened by Employee 16. 26.11.24 Ticket assigned REPORT 2: # TRAIN CE / ADFS Integration: CE Train Server, ADFS Client Registration, and Toolbox MFA This community centers on the TRAIN CE/CE Train server integration that relies on on‑premises ADFS for authentication. A newly registered ADFS client used by the Toolbox to orchestrate MFA had EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders enabled in PROD, which caused ADFS to issue JWTs and to forward MFA to external claims providers; those changes broke non‑browser integrations and produced widespread login/connectivity failures across environments. The incident affected the Train CE server, involved remediation actions (repair installation, federation metadata updates, certificate removals), and required coordinated troubleshooting by internal support engineers, the Identity/Architecture team and an external vendor (Avanade). Secondary factors observed during remediation included stale device compliance from hybrid Azure AD‑joined devices and Conditional Access evaluations that contributed to access variability for some users. The investigation produced both an ADFS configuration documentation artifact and guidance in a broader Identity & Authentication blueprint to prevent recurrence and to inform escalation and monitoring practices. ## Root cause: misconfigured ADFS client registration (EnableJWT + RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders) on a new client The investigation identified a new client application whose ADFS client registration had EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders set to true; this caused ADFS to issue JWTs and to delegate MFA to external claims providers, which the downstream Toolbox/Train integration did not expect and therefore failed to authenticate. The new client registration and the parameter choices are explicitly recorded as the root cause and were adjusted to match the TEST environment to resolve the failures [Data: Entities (249,248,240,241); Relationships (390,370)]. Recommendation: enforce a documented client‑registration review process that verifies token format and MFA delegation flags before promoting client registrations to PROD, and add a checklist item to the CI/CD or change request to confirm EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders settings for non‑browser integrations. ## Production impact and remediation steps applied to the CE Train server The CE Train server (TRAIN CE) experienced connectivity and authentication failures across users and environments; remediation steps recorded include a repair installation of the CE Train server (02.12.24), federation metadata updates on ADFS, and removal/rotation of old federation certificates—followed by ADFS parameter changes that restored correct behaviour. These actions and the target system are captured in the incident notes and ticket timeline [Data: Entities (252,235,254,253); Relationships (405,409,410,367)]. Recommendation: codify the validated remediation sequence (repair, federation metadata verification, certificate refresh, client parameter alignment) into a runbook for TRAIN CE integrations so responders can execute a repeatable playbook during future authentication incidents. ## Stakeholder and escalation map: support engineers, identity/architecture, application owners and external vendor involvement Multiple named stakeholders participated in triage and remediation: Employee 21 acted as lead analyst/owner for related incidents and executed configuration changes and device sync operations; Employee 14 reported and coordinated the initial ticket; Employee 24 and Admin appear in the ticket transfer chain; Avanade participated as an external troubleshooting partner; and the affected tenant and application owners are Customer Company 11AG / 11CE TRAIN. The ticket and assignment flows show cross‑team responsibility and vendor engagement during the incident [Data: Entities (232,4,231,10,229,230); Relationships (353,361,391,17,365)]. Recommendation: publish a concise stakeholder contact matrix and an escalation path (first responder/System Engineer -> Identity/Architecture -> Application Owner -> Vendor) and include roles, expected SLAs, and preferred communication channels for TRAIN CE‑class authentication incidents. ## Conditional Access and device sync were contributing factors for some access failures but were not the primary root cause Conditional Access was evaluated and did not present an outright blocking rule for the affected sign‑ins; however, some hybrid Azure AD‑joined devices reported stale or 'unknown' compliance for 6–8 hours, causing the 'Require compliant device' CA policy to evaluate as not satisfied and producing access variability for certain users. Employee 21 triggered manual device synchronization as part of remediation to update device compliance state faster [Data: Entities (683,680,688,684); Relationships (1199,1201,1206,1189)]. Recommendation: implement monitoring/alerts for device‑sync latency and include device synchronization health in incident triage dashboards; where appropriate, create temporary CA policy exceptions documented in change control but tied to a limited remediation window. ## Logging, monitoring and documentation gaps identified and addressed with configuration documentation and blueprint guidance The post‑incident artifacts include an ADFS configuration documentation entry capturing the corrected client registration settings and the investigation details, authored/confirmed by Employee 21, and a separate Identity & Authentication blueprint that prescribes decision trees, token format guidance, and escalation routes. The blueprint highlights the need for correlated logging across ADFS, claims providers and Entra ID to diagnose delegated MFA and JWT/SAML token flows [Data: Entities (244,232,207,219,208); Relationships (364,317,330)]. Recommendation: ensure the ADFS configuration documentation is versioned in the configuration management repository and update monitoring/alerting to collect token issuance events, claims‑provider logs and correlation IDs to shorten future diagnosis time. ## Token format and non‑browser flow mismatch risk (JWT vs SAML) for applications Enabling JWT issuance via EnableJWT changes the token format emitted by ADFS (JWT/OIDC) and requires that consuming applications support and validate JWTs; the blueprint warns that non‑browser integrations and service accounts commonly fail when token formats change unexpectedly. The incident demonstrates this risk where the new client caused JWT issuance that the Train integration could not process [Data: Entities (240,242,215,216); Relationships (379,324,334)]. Recommendation: require application owners to declare supported token formats in application inventories and gate any client registration change that switches token formats with explicit owner approval and a test plan validating non‑browser flows. ## Process and governance weakness: PROD‑only client registration change and insufficient pre‑deployment verification Investigators initially considered and briefly excluded ADFS because only PROD had been modified over the weekend; the error reproduced across environments, and only later was the misconfigured new client identified as the root cause—indicating a gap in change control or rollout validation for client registrations in PROD. The ticket timeline and decision notes record that the misconfigured registration was corrected to match TEST [Data: Entities (21,249,251); Relationships (65,228,390)]. Recommendation: implement formal change controls for ADFS client registrations (including peer review and a rollback plan), require a documented mapping between TEST and PROD registration parameters, and add pre‑promotion automated checks that detect risky flags like EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders in non‑browser client contexts. ## Operationalizing the Decision Tree and runbook: where to escalate and what telemetry to collect The Identity & Authentication blueprint includes a decision tree for authentication issues (question flow for browser vs non‑browser, token type checks, MFA delegation checks and escalation criteria). The incident suggests operationalizing that decision tree into an incident runbook covering when to escalate to the Identity/Architecture team, what logs to collect (ADFS token issuance, claims provider events, Entra ID sign‑in and sync logs), and what immediate mitigations to try (client parameter rollback, device sync, certificate checks) [Data: Entities (225,207,220,219); Relationships (322,348,330)]. Recommendation: convert the blueprint decision tree into a one‑page runbook with command snippets for log collection, prioritized diagnostics, and a checklist for safe client parameter changes to shorten mean‑time‑to‑resolution and reduce handoff friction.

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 64: # Azure AD Connect vs Cloud COMPLIANCE_TEAM — External Guest Access Loss (SharePoint) This community centers on a cloud-native COMPLIANCE_TEAM group and an On‑Prem COMPLIANCE_TEAM group that share a display name but have distinct ObjectIDs. Azure AD Connect synchronized the On‑Prem group into Entra ID/Azure AD, which progressively replaced or caused SharePoint to evaluate the synchronized On‑Prem object instead of the cloud-native group. Because external guest accounts lived only in the cloud-native group (they were not present On‑Prem), those external guests lost SharePoint access while internal users (present in the On‑Prem group) retained access. A system engineer reproduced the issue, used Microsoft Graph and the Entra ID portal to compare group membership and source attributes, and guided remediation steps including consolidation/cleanup and a full sync to restore intended access mappings. [Data: Entities (265,266,267,268,269,270); Relationships (437,440,441,445,446,435,430)] ## Root cause: Azure AD Connect synchronization caused the cloud-native group to be overwritten/evaluated as the On‑Prem synchronized object, removing external guests from effective membership Investigators observed that Azure AD Connect gradually overwrote or deactivated the cloud-native COMPLIANCE_TEAM group with the synchronized On‑Prem group (same display name, different Object IDs), which changed the effective membership evaluated by SharePoint and resulted in external guests losing access. This behavior is documented in the service description for Azure AD Connect and the relationship between the cloud and On‑Prem group objects. [Data: Entities (269,265,266); Relationships (437,440,446)] The critical aspect is the display-name collision combined with synchronization semantics: SharePoint was referencing the synchronized On‑Prem object (the object with the On‑Prem ObjectID), not the separate cloud-native group that contained guest members, so membership evaluation excluded cloud-only guest accounts. This mismatch between object identity and display name is the immediate mechanism of access loss. [Data: Entities (265,266,267); Relationships (437,444,435)] ## Scope and impact: External guests lost access while internal users remained unaffected External project members provisioned as Guest accounts in Entra ID/Azure AD lost SharePoint access because they were members of the cloud-native COMPLIANCE_TEAM, which was distinct from the synchronized On‑Prem group used by SharePoint. Internal users were members of the On‑Prem COMPLIANCE_TEAM and therefore continued to have access. This differential outcome is captured by the entity relationships showing guest membership in the cloud group and internal membership in the On‑Prem group. [Data: Entities (267,268,265,266); Relationships (437,441)] Investigators also noted that affected accounts had differing "source" attributes visible in the Entra ID portal; examining the source attribute helped correlate which accounts originated On‑Prem versus in the cloud, which was instrumental in identifying why some users were impacted and others were not. This observation ties the access symptoms back to identity source metadata in Entra ID. [Data: Entities (270,267); Relationships (445)] ## Investigation and diagnostics used: System Engineer 14, Microsoft Graph, and Entra ID inspection A system engineer conducted in-depth analysis: reviewing Azure AD Connect behavior, inspecting group membership via Microsoft Graph, reproducing the issue in test, and guiding remediation (consolidation, cleanup, full sync). The engineer used Microsoft Graph queries to enumerate members of both the cloud-native and synchronized On‑Prem groups to compare differences in member lists. These investigative actions are documented in the entity and relationship records. [Data: Entities (261,271,269); Relationships (430,431)] The Entra ID portal was used to observe account "source" attributes (e.g., "source = Windows Server AD" vs "source = Azure AD"), providing direct evidence to distinguish which accounts were cloud-native guests and which were synchronized On‑Prem accounts. This combination of Graph API and portal inspection provided the necessary diagnostics for root-cause confirmation. [Data: Entities (270,267); Relationships (445,431)] ## Service dependency: SharePoint permission construct (SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN) referenced the COMPLIANCE_TEAM group, creating an access dependency SharePoint's permission construct SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN was found to contain or reference the COMPLIANCE_TEAM group; investigators examined membership mappings between these constructs during the analysis. Because SharePoint evaluated the group object that Azure AD Connect synchronized, the SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN -> COMPLIANCE_TEAM linkage is the downstream dependency that surfaced the access failure for external guests. [Data: Entities (264,265,266); Relationships (435)] This highlights that access control in SharePoint depended on the directory object identity (ObjectID) rather than purely on display name. Any change in which directory object is presented to SharePoint (for example, due to synchronization overwrite) will directly affect access evaluation for resources protected by SP_COMPLIANCE_MAIN. [Data: Relationships (435,446)] ## Identity object collision risk: same display name, different ObjectIDs created a deterministic failure mode There were two distinct COMPLIANCE_TEAM objects: a cloud-native Azure AD group and an On‑Prem AD group synchronized by Azure AD Connect. Both used the same display name but had different ObjectIDs; when the synchronized On‑Prem object replaced or was used by downstream services, cloud-only members were excluded. This naming/collision pattern created a reproducible failure mode tied directly to synchronization behavior. [Data: Entities (265,266); Relationships (444,437,440)] Because Azure AD Connect will surface synchronized objects into Entra ID and object identity is the authoritative binding for resource access, organizations must treat display-name collisions between cloud-native and On‑Prem groups as high risk; this incident demonstrates how that risk materializes as access loss for cloud-only accounts. [Data: Relationships (446,437)] ## Remediation performed: consolidation, cleanup, and a full sync guided by the system engineer restored intended mappings The system engineer guided remediation actions including consolidation and cleanup of the conflicting group objects and performed a full sync to restore the correct group membership and access behavior. The ticket notes that a full synchronization was performed as part of remediation and that the engineer reproduced the issue in test to validate the fix. These documented steps indicate the operational path taken to remediate the outage. [Data: Entities (261,269); Relationships (430)] While the immediate remediation restored access, the records do not include a formal postmortem or a permanent governance control to prevent recurrence; the cleanup and full sync were corrective but the underlying naming/ownership risk remains unless addressed by policy. The available data supports the remediation actions but does not yet show a persistent mitigation mechanism. [Data: Entities (269,261); Relationships (430,437)] ## Governance and monitoring gaps: lack of explicit evidence of group ownership, naming policies, or sync exclusion rules The corpus documents the synchronization collision, diagnostic steps, and corrective actions, but it contains no explicit records describing group ownership assignments, naming conventions to prevent display-name collisions, or Azure AD Connect exclusion/filtering rules applied to groups to prevent cloud-native objects from being overwritten. The absence of these governance artifacts in the provided records indicates potential control gaps that allowed the condition to occur. [Data: Entities (265,266,269); Relationships (437,446,435)] Because SharePoint permission constructs referenced the group object identity and Azure AD Connect can alter which object is authoritative in the cloud, formal controls (group ownership, naming conventions, synchronization scoping/exclusions, and monitoring/alerting for object conflicts) are required to reduce recurrence risk. REPORT 4: # KF10.com Domain Controllers and Microsoft Defender for Identity Sensors The community centers on Active Directory Domain Controllers (on‑prem and Azure) with Microsoft Defender for Identity sensors installed; sensors run on the DC hosts, send telemetry to the Defender Cloud Service over outbound HTTPS (TCP 443), and depend on a chain of network and host controls (Windows Firewall, perimeter firewall, Azure NSG), TLS/certificate validity, proxy configuration and local security software. The environment is governed by a written architecture/operational standard and a decision tree/troubleshooting flow that prescribes checks for service state, connectivity (DNS/443), event logs, TLS/proxy, and reinstall/monitor steps. Disruptions to any of these dependencies cause sensors to report “unhealthy”, reduce or eliminate health alerts in the cloud, and create blind spots in AD authentication/replication monitoring. [Data: Entities (696,697,698,702,699); Relationships (1231,1235,1255,1253,1258)] ## Domain Controllers and Defender sensors are tightly coupled and form the core monitoring plane Defender sensors run directly on Domain Controllers and capture authentication (Kerberos/NTLM), LDAP and replication telemetry; DC health and sensor behavior are tightly coupled, making the DCs and sensors a single operational surface that must be maintained together. The standard and operational guidance explicitly treat DCs with sensors as the primary subject for architecture and operations. This coupling means DC outages, host‑level changes, or misconfigured sensors directly degrade AD security visibility. [Data: Entities (696,697), Relationships (1231,1245,1246,1228)] ## Outbound HTTPS (TCP 443) to the Defender Cloud is a single critical network dependency Sensors rely on outbound HTTPS to send telemetry to the Defender Cloud Service; blocking or filtering TCP 443 prevents telemetry delivery and causes sensors to be marked unhealthy. The relationship between sensors and the cloud service is explicit and the document lists outbound HTTPS as a mandatory requirement. Network edge/firewall rules and Azure NSGs are singled out as controls that can interrupt this egress, which means perimeter and cloud egress policies are a primary root cause class for multi‑DC connectivity failures. [Data: Entities (702,698), Relationships (1235,1255,1258)] ## Host and perimeter network controls (Windows Firewall, perimeter firewall, Azure NSG) are frequent gating factors On‑host Windows Firewall settings on on‑prem DCs, perimeter firewall rules at the network edge, and Azure NSG rules for cloud DCs are all called out as required to permit sensor outbound connections and internal AD ports. Misconfiguration anywhere in this chain can simultaneously block telemetry and break AD protocols (LDAP/Kerberos/RPC), producing both service impact and monitoring blind spots. This indicates a need for coordinated change control across host, network, and cloud teams when modifying firewall/NSG rules. [Data: Entities (699,700,701,703), Relationships (1253,1255,1258,1261)] ## TLS/certificate problems, proxy authentication, and internal security software are persistent secondary failure modes The document lists TLS/certificate issues as an item that can prevent secure HTTPS between sensors and Microsoft endpoints; it also calls out proxy configuration/authentication and internal security software (host or inline appliances) as causes that can block or interfere with sensor egress. TLS mismatches or policy changes can cause failed egress despite port availability, while proxies or local security controls may require explicit exceptions or credentials for the sensors. These are common categories of intermittent or oscillating healthy/unhealthy sensor reports and should be included in root‑cause workflows. [Data: Entities (705,704,714), Relationships (1242,1241,1249,1260)] ## Sensor operational state, event logs and the decision tree are the primary diagnostic artefacts Operational checks emphasize verifying the sensor service state (running/reinstall), reviewing local sensor and system event logs, and following the decision tree that differentiates single‑DC vs multi‑DC issues. The decision tree maps to concrete troubleshooting steps: service state, outbound connectivity/DNS, proxy/TLS checks, logs and reinstall guidance; the Defender Service Health Portal is recommended to separate tenant/local problems from Microsoft service outages. These diagnostic artefacts should be centrally collected and accessible to on‑call teams for faster triage. [Data: Entities (708,712,709,706), Relationships (1243,1247,1264,1263)] ## Unhealthy sensors lead to missing or delayed health alerts and create security visibility gaps Sensor telemetry feeds the health/alerting pipeline in the Defender Cloud Service; when sensors are unhealthy or cannot reach the cloud, health alerts are absent or delayed, reducing detection capability for authentication anomalies and replication issues. This is a direct security and compliance exposure because AD authentication telemetry is critical for detecting lateral movement and credential misuse in the environment. The linkage between sensor connectivity and alert production is explicitly documented. [Data: Entities (713,698), Relationships (1248,1235)] ## Standards and the architecture document exist but network/topology change control is an elevated risk area An architecture and operational standard (Active Directory Domain Controller & Microsoft Defender for Identity Standard V1.0) and a decision tree/troubleshooting flow are documented, prescribing required ports and operational checks. However, the document specifically requires review of network segment/topology changes because such changes can alter the effective scope of perimeter firewall rules and NSGs, potentially blocking sensor traffic. This identifies network/topology change management as a governance gap to enforce when changes are planned. [Data: Entities (695,709,707), Relationships (1228,1256)] ## Priority remediation and operational recommendations align directly to documented checks Immediate high‑value actions are to: (1) verify outbound HTTPS (TCP 443) egress from affected DCs (host and perimeter/NSG), (2) confirm the sensor service is running and review sensor/system event logs, (3) validate TLS/certificate validity and proxy authentication/whitelisting, and (4) consult the Defender Service Health Portal to rule out cloud service issues. These steps are the same checks defined in the decision tree and should be codified into an escalation runbook and enforced via change control for firewall/NSG and topology changes to avoid recurrence. Implementing centralized log collection and automated health monitoring for sensor state would reduce MTTR. [Data: Entities (702,708,705,709), Relationships (1255,1243,1242,1263)]

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 13: # KF40 Portal Publication (portal.kf40.ch) — WAF/Reverse-Proxy and Firewall Operational Community This community centers on the perimeter publishing stack (WAF / Reverse Proxy) that publishes the Customer Company 40 portal (portal.kf40.ch) and the network/firewall estate that supports user connectivity (including Teams media). A high‑priority support ticket (TI-1594408) documented an external 502 Bad Gateway outage for portal.kf40.ch while internal access remained functional. Troubleshooting showed the backend application servers and load balancer were healthy, TLS certificates were validated and not the root cause, and the outage correlated to a weekend security patch/change that reloaded a Default-Strict inspection/profile and removed a custom header rewrite rule needed to present X-Forwarded-Proto: https to the backend. Restoring the Portal-App-Template / custom header rewrite configuration per the Secure Web Publishing blueprint resolved external access. Separately, a Zurich site firewall hardening change restricted outbound UDP to ports 3478–3481 only, blocking the dynamic RTP port range and causing one‑way/no audio in Teams; a temporary port opening restored media flows and confirmed the root cause. The incident and artifacts highlight technical, process, and governance gaps around post‑patch template/profile validation, change control for perimeter devices, and runbooks for header/HTTP profile preservation during TLS inspection/termination. ## Root cause of portal 502: WAF TLS inspection plus missing/custom header rewrite rule (X-Forwarded-Proto) prevented the backend from seeing HTTPS. External requests to portal.kf40.ch returned 502 Bad Gateway while internal access remained functional; investigation showed the reverse proxy/WAF performs TLS termination/inspection and is expected to forward X-Forwarded-Proto: https to the backend, but that header was not present when inspection was active, causing the backend to misinterpret the request and return errors. This causal chain is documented in the incident artifacts linking the REVERSE PROXY / WAF to the APPLICATION SERVER and noting the missing CUSTOM HEADER REWRITE RULE and absent X-FORWARDED-PROTO header as drivers for the 502s [Data: Entities (302,312,319); Relationships (492,499,496)]. Restoring the header rewrite behavior per the Portal-App-Template/blueprint returned external functionality, confirming the header rewrite absence (not the backend or certificate validity) was the functional trigger for failures [Data: Entities (311,300); Relationships (568,499)]. ## Security patch/template reload replaced Portal-App-Template with Default-Strict, causing template/profile drift and header removal. A weekend security patch caused the reverse proxy/WAF to reload a default inspection/profile (Default-Strict) which replaced the custom PORTAL-APP-TEMPLATE and its documented HTTP/profile/header‑rewrite rules; the activated Default-Strict profile altered header-handling and removed or failed to preserve X-Forwarded headers, reproducing the 502 when TLS inspection was enabled [Data: Entities (310,311); Relationships (497,568,548)]. This shows configuration/template drift introduced by patching—where a device reset to a default profile overwrote application‑specific templates—rather than an application or certificate error, creating a clear process risk for perimeter devices that host per-application templates [Data: Relationships (497,562)]. ## Backend and load‑balancing layer were healthy—issue isolated to publishing/inspection layer. Load balancer health checks and internal tests marked the backend application server as healthy and reachable, indicating the backend process was not the primary failure point; monitoring and health checks were used to validate stability after the fix before ticket closure [Data: Entities (305,318,308); Relationships (504,505,513)]. These diagnostics materially constrained the fault domain to the perimeter (WAF/reverse proxy) and saved time by directing remediation to template/profile/header handling rather than application restarts alone [Data: Relationships (492,563)]. ## TLS certificate change was investigated and ruled out as the root cause. The customer reported a recently installed TLS certificate and helpdesk validated the certificate CN, SAN and chain; while the certificate change was temporally proximate to the outage, diagnostics showed certificate validity/chain issues were not the cause of the 502s—the publishing layer header behavior was [Data: Entities (306); Relationships (486)]. The certificate service and blueprint decision tree were consulted as part of the normal checklist, which helped rule TLS problems out and focus on WAF behavior [Data: Relationships (552,508)]. ## Teams media outage at Zurich caused by outbound firewall restriction blocking dynamic RTP ports. A separate but contemporaneous operational change at the Zurich site implemented a security-hardening outbound rule that permitted only UDP 3478–3481, blocking the dynamic RTP port range required for Teams media when Media Bypass is used. This change produced no audio for external calls; temporarily opening the wider UDP/RTP range restored audio immediately, confirming the firewall rule as the root cause [Data: Entities (303,442,443); Relationships (739,741,742,733)]. Firewall logs and change records tied the timing of the outbound restriction to the onset of audio issues, showing this was a local change management failure with direct impact to voice/media availability [Data: Relationships (738,739)]. ## Blueprint and operational docs exist but were not enforced by the post‑patch validation process. An authoritative Secure Web Publishing blueprint and versioned guidance (BLUEPRINT: SECURE WEB PUBLISHING – KF40 – VERSION 3.2 / WEB PUBLISHING BLUEPRINT) defines required behaviors: TLS termination at the WAF, required Portal-App-Template, and documented header rewrite rules and validation after patches. The team retrieved the blueprint from DMS to align remediation, indicating awareness of the standard; however, the blueprint's requirement to validate WAF profiles and preserve custom rewrite rules was not enforced automatically during the patch that reset profiles, producing configuration drift [Data: Entities (307,339,356); Relationships (508,512,562)]. This gap between blueprint and operational enforcement—where a profile reload caused a prohibited Default-Strict to be applied despite the blueprint forbidding it—exposes the environment to repeat regressions unless validation and change controls are hardened [Data: Relationships (548,497)]. ## Operational ownership, escalation path, and remediation were used correctly but could be formalized into runbooks and automated checks. The high-priority ticket TI-1594408 was owned initially by Employee 29 (helpdesk) who performed initial checks and escalated to BU Network & Security; BU Network & Security then took ownership and applied WAF/reverse proxy configuration changes to restore service, with monitoring confirming 24 hours stability before closure—showing correct functional escalation and remediation occurred [Data: Entities (295,298,299); Relationships (479,487,476)]. The artifacts show manual steps and handoffs (tests, template restores, monitoring) that resolved the incident, but the manual nature of these actions recommends formalizing a post‑patch WAF validation runbook, automated configuration drift detection, and explicit pre/post patch checklists to reduce mean‑time‑to‑repair and recurrence risk [Data: Relationships (479,513)].
