context: REPORT 15: # Basel Print Infrastructure — Print Server, MFP-BASEL-01 & Printer VLAN The community centers on centralized Windows print servers that host location-based printer queues and enforce queue-level permissions via Active Directory groups and Group Policy; physical printers (including MFP-BASEL-01) are deployed into a dedicated PRINTER VLAN that requires inter-VLAN routing and firewall/ACL rules so the print server and client workstations can reach them. An operational incident (TI-1622483) combined a misconfigured explicit Deny ACE on the MFP-BASEL-01 queue with recent network segmentation that initially prevented the print server from reaching the printer until routing/firewall rules were corrected; remediation removed the explicit Deny, corrected AD/GPO configuration and adjusted network rules according to the Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard and Decision Tree guidance. Key artifacts that govern behavior and troubleshooting are the Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard and an embedded Decision Tree which prescribe AD-group-based provisioning, GPO-based default printers, SMTP relay restrictions for printer VLANs, and a diagnostic checklist covering spooler, queue permissions and VLAN reachability. [Data: Entities (70,52,137,53,427); Relationships (112,119,704,222,695)] ## Canonical architecture: central Windows print servers + VLAN‑isolated printers, mapped by AD/GPO Printing is implemented as a centralized service: Windows print servers host queues and standardized drivers, and queues are mapped to users via AD groups and Group Policy (location-based queues and defaults). This is the foundational design documented in the Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard and implemented via GPOs and provisioning processes. [Data: Entities (70,718,53,57); Relationships (112,92)] Because printers are placed in a dedicated PRINTER VLAN, reachability depends on inter-VLAN routing and firewall/ACL rules; the standard requires routing so the print server can reach the printers and instructs operators to validate VLAN reachability (ping) as part of diagnostics. This separation centralizes printer topology but creates a dependency between print services and network configuration that must be validated on every VLAN change. [Data: Entities (137,1280); Relationships (715,1312)] ## Incident root cause: overlapping queue-level DENY ACE plus network segmentation The production outage reported in TI-1622483 manifested as an affected user receiving immediate “Access denied” errors while other users' jobs either remained in the queue or disappeared without printing. Troubleshooting identified two correlated root causes: an explicit Deny ACE on the MFP-BASEL-01 queue that overrode group-based Allow permissions, and the printer’s move into a separate PRINTER VLAN that initially prevented the print server from reaching the device until routing/firewall rules were adjusted. Both issues were necessary to explain the mixed symptoms observed. [Data: Entities (73,52,71,137); Relationships (119,704)] This demonstrates a classic dual-failure mode where identity/ACL misconfiguration blocks a single principal while network segmentation blocks server-to-device delivery for other paths — both must be checked in parallel when jobs appear then disappear. [Data: Relationships (704,119)] ## Remediation followed standards: Deny removed, AD/GPO corrected, firewall/routing adjusted Resolution actions documented in the ticket included removal of the explicit Deny ACE on the printer queue, correction of AD group membership and forcing a Group Policy refresh so location-based defaults applied correctly, and adjustment of the firewall/routing rule to allow the print server to reach the PRINTER VLAN. The ticket closure explicitly cites configuration changes implemented according to the Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard. These combined changes restored normal behavior and the customer confirmed functionality. [Data: Entities (427,73,53); Relationships (80,695)] The remediation path aligns to the Decision Tree’s prescribed sequence (permission checks, GPO refresh, VLAN reachability tests) and is therefore repeatable and auditable when executed against the standard. [Data: Entities (164); Relationships (1312,1311)] ## Operational decision-tree and diagnostics are present and were leveraged An operational Decision Tree and Use Cases document exists to drive consistent troubleshooting (checklist of spooler state, queue visibility, AD membership, VLAN reachability, paused queues, and test prints). The decision tree specifically includes steps for VLAN reachability/ping, paused-queue checks, and print‑job presence/absence to route diagnostics. This artifact was used to guide verification and is a key enabler of consistent incident handling. [Data: Entities (164); Relationships (1312,1311,1310)] Maintaining and enforcing use of this decision tree reduces mean time to repair by ensuring both identity/permission and network segments are validated early in the workflow. ## GPO/AD design fragility: overlapping or parallel groups produce incorrect default mappings Group Policy assigns location-based default printers by evaluating AD group membership; overlapping or parallel AD groups have previously caused the Zurich printer to be applied to Basel users until memberships and GPO targeting were corrected. The environment relies on single-purpose AD groups (no parallel groups) per the provisioning rules in the standard, and deviations here directly cause incorrect printer mapping and user experience issues. [Data: Entities (57,64); Relationships (92,88)] Recommendation: enforce strict group-naming and lifecycle controls, validate nested memberships during change windows, and include membership checks in the GPO deployment runbook to prevent cross-location mapping errors. ## SMTP/scan‑to‑mail compliance gap for new PRINTER VLAN The PRINTER VLAN’s IP range had not been added to the organization’s SMTP relay allow list when the VLAN was created, causing external SMTP relay attempts from devices in that VLAN to be denied until the range was whitelisted. The Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard prohibits printers sending directly to the Internet and requires usage of a dedicated SMTP relay whose access list is maintained. This is a non-printing impact that affects scan‑to‑mail functionality and compliance with the standard. [Data: Entities (137,53); Relationships (222)] Network and change-management checklists must include updates to SMTP relay ACLs when introducing new device VLANs to avoid secondary outages impacting scan workflows. ## Monitoring and diagnostics gap: spooler state alone is insufficient — include queue ACL and VLAN checks The Print Spooler service on the server was observed to be active while print jobs were not reaching the printer; restarting the spooler can transiently change symptoms but does not correct queue‑level ACLs or network routing problems. The decision tree explicitly separates spooler health from queue status and VLAN reachability checks, indicating the need for multi-dimensional monitoring and alerting (service health + queue ACL anomalies + inter-VLAN reachability). [Data: Entities (159,429); Relationships (255,1312)] Operationally, alerts that only surface a running spooler but declining print completion rates should automatically trigger queue-ACL and VLAN-reachability checks as part of the incident playbook. ## Clear stakeholder boundaries but ad hoc coordination required: IT Infrastructure, Network Team, site contacts Document ownership and operational responsibilities are defined: IT Infrastructure authors and enforces the Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard and provisions printers; the Network Team is responsible for VLAN configuration and firewall changes and was engaged to adjust the firewall rule that blocked server-to-printer traffic; the ticket lists a site contact who confirmed restoration. However, VLAN and SMTP ACL changes show that coordination between these groups must be formalized in the change process to prevent related failures. [Data: Entities (717,428,427); Relationships (712,222,695)] Recommendation: define explicit escalation and approval paths for changes that affect both identity/provisioning and network segmentation, include cross-team sign-off in the change template, and publish a short runbook for combined print-network incidents. REPORT 13: # KF40 Portal Publication (portal.kf40.ch) — WAF/Reverse-Proxy and Firewall Operational Community This community centers on the perimeter publishing stack (WAF / Reverse Proxy) that publishes the Customer Company 40 portal (portal.kf40.ch) and the network/firewall estate that supports user connectivity (including Teams media). A high‑priority support ticket (TI-1594408) documented an external 502 Bad Gateway outage for portal.kf40.ch while internal access remained functional. Troubleshooting showed the backend application servers and load balancer were healthy, TLS certificates were validated and not the root cause, and the outage correlated to a weekend security patch/change that reloaded a Default-Strict inspection/profile and removed a custom header rewrite rule needed to present X-Forwarded-Proto: https to the backend. Restoring the Portal-App-Template / custom header rewrite configuration per the Secure Web Publishing blueprint resolved external access. Separately, a Zurich site firewall hardening change restricted outbound UDP to ports 3478–3481 only, blocking the dynamic RTP port range and causing one‑way/no audio in Teams; a temporary port opening restored media flows and confirmed the root cause. The incident and artifacts highlight technical, process, and governance gaps around post‑patch template/profile validation, change control for perimeter devices, and runbooks for header/HTTP profile preservation during TLS inspection/termination. ## Root cause of portal 502: WAF TLS inspection plus missing/custom header rewrite rule (X-Forwarded-Proto) prevented the backend from seeing HTTPS. External requests to portal.kf40.ch returned 502 Bad Gateway while internal access remained functional; investigation showed the reverse proxy/WAF performs TLS termination/inspection and is expected to forward X-Forwarded-Proto: https to the backend, but that header was not present when inspection was active, causing the backend to misinterpret the request and return errors. This causal chain is documented in the incident artifacts linking the REVERSE PROXY / WAF to the APPLICATION SERVER and noting the missing CUSTOM HEADER REWRITE RULE and absent X-FORWARDED-PROTO header as drivers for the 502s [Data: Entities (302,312,319); Relationships (492,499,496)]. Restoring the header rewrite behavior per the Portal-App-Template/blueprint returned external functionality, confirming the header rewrite absence (not the backend or certificate validity) was the functional trigger for failures [Data: Entities (311,300); Relationships (568,499)]. ## Security patch/template reload replaced Portal-App-Template with Default-Strict, causing template/profile drift and header removal. A weekend security patch caused the reverse proxy/WAF to reload a default inspection/profile (Default-Strict) which replaced the custom PORTAL-APP-TEMPLATE and its documented HTTP/profile/header‑rewrite rules; the activated Default-Strict profile altered header-handling and removed or failed to preserve X-Forwarded headers, reproducing the 502 when TLS inspection was enabled [Data: Entities (310,311); Relationships (497,568,548)]. This shows configuration/template drift introduced by patching—where a device reset to a default profile overwrote application‑specific templates—rather than an application or certificate error, creating a clear process risk for perimeter devices that host per-application templates [Data: Relationships (497,562)]. ## Backend and load‑balancing layer were healthy—issue isolated to publishing/inspection layer. Load balancer health checks and internal tests marked the backend application server as healthy and reachable, indicating the backend process was not the primary failure point; monitoring and health checks were used to validate stability after the fix before ticket closure [Data: Entities (305,318,308); Relationships (504,505,513)]. These diagnostics materially constrained the fault domain to the perimeter (WAF/reverse proxy) and saved time by directing remediation to template/profile/header handling rather than application restarts alone [Data: Relationships (492,563)]. ## TLS certificate change was investigated and ruled out as the root cause. The customer reported a recently installed TLS certificate and helpdesk validated the certificate CN, SAN and chain; while the certificate change was temporally proximate to the outage, diagnostics showed certificate validity/chain issues were not the cause of the 502s—the publishing layer header behavior was [Data: Entities (306); Relationships (486)]. The certificate service and blueprint decision tree were consulted as part of the normal checklist, which helped rule TLS problems out and focus on WAF behavior [Data: Relationships (552,508)]. ## Teams media outage at Zurich caused by outbound firewall restriction blocking dynamic RTP ports. A separate but contemporaneous operational change at the Zurich site implemented a security-hardening outbound rule that permitted only UDP 3478–3481, blocking the dynamic RTP port range required for Teams media when Media Bypass is used. This change produced no audio for external calls; temporarily opening the wider UDP/RTP range restored audio immediately, confirming the firewall rule as the root cause [Data: Entities (303,442,443); Relationships (739,741,742,733)]. Firewall logs and change records tied the timing of the outbound restriction to the onset of audio issues, showing this was a local change management failure with direct impact to voice/media availability [Data: Relationships (738,739)]. ## Blueprint and operational docs exist but were not enforced by the post‑patch validation process. An authoritative Secure Web Publishing blueprint and versioned guidance (BLUEPRINT: SECURE WEB PUBLISHING – KF40 – VERSION 3.2 / WEB PUBLISHING BLUEPRINT) defines required behaviors: TLS termination at the WAF, required Portal-App-Template, and documented header rewrite rules and validation after patches. The team retrieved the blueprint from DMS to align remediation, indicating awareness of the standard; however, the blueprint's requirement to validate WAF profiles and preserve custom rewrite rules was not enforced automatically during the patch that reset profiles, producing configuration drift [Data: Entities (307,339,356); Relationships (508,512,562)]. This gap between blueprint and operational enforcement—where a profile reload caused a prohibited Default-Strict to be applied despite the blueprint forbidding it—exposes the environment to repeat regressions unless validation and change controls are hardened [Data: Relationships (548,497)]. ## Operational ownership, escalation path, and remediation were used correctly but could be formalized into runbooks and automated checks. The high-priority ticket TI-1594408 was owned initially by Employee 29 (helpdesk) who performed initial checks and escalated to BU Network & Security; BU Network & Security then took ownership and applied WAF/reverse proxy configuration changes to restore service, with monitoring confirming 24 hours stability before closure—showing correct functional escalation and remediation occurred [Data: Entities (295,298,299); Relationships (479,487,476)]. The artifacts show manual steps and handoffs (tests, template restores, monitoring) that resolved the incident, but the manual nature of these actions recommends formalizing a post‑patch WAF validation runbook, automated configuration drift detection, and explicit pre/post patch checklists to reduce mean‑time‑to‑repair and recurrence risk [Data: Relationships (479,513)]. REPORT 2: # TRAIN CE / ADFS Integration: CE Train Server, ADFS Client Registration, and Toolbox MFA This community centers on the TRAIN CE/CE Train server integration that relies on on‑premises ADFS for authentication. A newly registered ADFS client used by the Toolbox to orchestrate MFA had EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders enabled in PROD, which caused ADFS to issue JWTs and to forward MFA to external claims providers; those changes broke non‑browser integrations and produced widespread login/connectivity failures across environments. The incident affected the Train CE server, involved remediation actions (repair installation, federation metadata updates, certificate removals), and required coordinated troubleshooting by internal support engineers, the Identity/Architecture team and an external vendor (Avanade). Secondary factors observed during remediation included stale device compliance from hybrid Azure AD‑joined devices and Conditional Access evaluations that contributed to access variability for some users. The investigation produced both an ADFS configuration documentation artifact and guidance in a broader Identity & Authentication blueprint to prevent recurrence and to inform escalation and monitoring practices. ## Root cause: misconfigured ADFS client registration (EnableJWT + RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders) on a new client The investigation identified a new client application whose ADFS client registration had EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders set to true; this caused ADFS to issue JWTs and to delegate MFA to external claims providers, which the downstream Toolbox/Train integration did not expect and therefore failed to authenticate. The new client registration and the parameter choices are explicitly recorded as the root cause and were adjusted to match the TEST environment to resolve the failures [Data: Entities (249,248,240,241); Relationships (390,370)]. Recommendation: enforce a documented client‑registration review process that verifies token format and MFA delegation flags before promoting client registrations to PROD, and add a checklist item to the CI/CD or change request to confirm EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders settings for non‑browser integrations. ## Production impact and remediation steps applied to the CE Train server The CE Train server (TRAIN CE) experienced connectivity and authentication failures across users and environments; remediation steps recorded include a repair installation of the CE Train server (02.12.24), federation metadata updates on ADFS, and removal/rotation of old federation certificates—followed by ADFS parameter changes that restored correct behaviour. These actions and the target system are captured in the incident notes and ticket timeline [Data: Entities (252,235,254,253); Relationships (405,409,410,367)]. Recommendation: codify the validated remediation sequence (repair, federation metadata verification, certificate refresh, client parameter alignment) into a runbook for TRAIN CE integrations so responders can execute a repeatable playbook during future authentication incidents. ## Stakeholder and escalation map: support engineers, identity/architecture, application owners and external vendor involvement Multiple named stakeholders participated in triage and remediation: Employee 21 acted as lead analyst/owner for related incidents and executed configuration changes and device sync operations; Employee 14 reported and coordinated the initial ticket; Employee 24 and Admin appear in the ticket transfer chain; Avanade participated as an external troubleshooting partner; and the affected tenant and application owners are Customer Company 11AG / 11CE TRAIN. The ticket and assignment flows show cross‑team responsibility and vendor engagement during the incident [Data: Entities (232,4,231,10,229,230); Relationships (353,361,391,17,365)]. Recommendation: publish a concise stakeholder contact matrix and an escalation path (first responder/System Engineer -> Identity/Architecture -> Application Owner -> Vendor) and include roles, expected SLAs, and preferred communication channels for TRAIN CE‑class authentication incidents. ## Conditional Access and device sync were contributing factors for some access failures but were not the primary root cause Conditional Access was evaluated and did not present an outright blocking rule for the affected sign‑ins; however, some hybrid Azure AD‑joined devices reported stale or 'unknown' compliance for 6–8 hours, causing the 'Require compliant device' CA policy to evaluate as not satisfied and producing access variability for certain users. Employee 21 triggered manual device synchronization as part of remediation to update device compliance state faster [Data: Entities (683,680,688,684); Relationships (1199,1201,1206,1189)]. Recommendation: implement monitoring/alerts for device‑sync latency and include device synchronization health in incident triage dashboards; where appropriate, create temporary CA policy exceptions documented in change control but tied to a limited remediation window. ## Logging, monitoring and documentation gaps identified and addressed with configuration documentation and blueprint guidance The post‑incident artifacts include an ADFS configuration documentation entry capturing the corrected client registration settings and the investigation details, authored/confirmed by Employee 21, and a separate Identity & Authentication blueprint that prescribes decision trees, token format guidance, and escalation routes. The blueprint highlights the need for correlated logging across ADFS, claims providers and Entra ID to diagnose delegated MFA and JWT/SAML token flows [Data: Entities (244,232,207,219,208); Relationships (364,317,330)]. Recommendation: ensure the ADFS configuration documentation is versioned in the configuration management repository and update monitoring/alerting to collect token issuance events, claims‑provider logs and correlation IDs to shorten future diagnosis time. ## Token format and non‑browser flow mismatch risk (JWT vs SAML) for applications Enabling JWT issuance via EnableJWT changes the token format emitted by ADFS (JWT/OIDC) and requires that consuming applications support and validate JWTs; the blueprint warns that non‑browser integrations and service accounts commonly fail when token formats change unexpectedly. The incident demonstrates this risk where the new client caused JWT issuance that the Train integration could not process [Data: Entities (240,242,215,216); Relationships (379,324,334)]. Recommendation: require application owners to declare supported token formats in application inventories and gate any client registration change that switches token formats with explicit owner approval and a test plan validating non‑browser flows. ## Process and governance weakness: PROD‑only client registration change and insufficient pre‑deployment verification Investigators initially considered and briefly excluded ADFS because only PROD had been modified over the weekend; the error reproduced across environments, and only later was the misconfigured new client identified as the root cause—indicating a gap in change control or rollout validation for client registrations in PROD. The ticket timeline and decision notes record that the misconfigured registration was corrected to match TEST [Data: Entities (21,249,251); Relationships (65,228,390)]. Recommendation: implement formal change controls for ADFS client registrations (including peer review and a rollback plan), require a documented mapping between TEST and PROD registration parameters, and add pre‑promotion automated checks that detect risky flags like EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders in non‑browser client contexts. ## Operationalizing the Decision Tree and runbook: where to escalate and what telemetry to collect The Identity & Authentication blueprint includes a decision tree for authentication issues (question flow for browser vs non‑browser, token type checks, MFA delegation checks and escalation criteria). The incident suggests operationalizing that decision tree into an incident runbook covering when to escalate to the Identity/Architecture team, what logs to collect (ADFS token issuance, claims provider events, Entra ID sign‑in and sync logs), and what immediate mitigations to try (client parameter rollback, device sync, certificate checks) [Data: Entities (225,207,220,219); Relationships (322,348,330)]. Recommendation: convert the blueprint decision tree into a one‑page runbook with command snippets for log collection, prioritized diagnostics, and a checklist for safe client parameter changes to shorten mean‑time‑to‑resolution and reduce handoff friction.

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 13: # KF40 Portal Publication (portal.kf40.ch) — WAF/Reverse-Proxy and Firewall Operational Community This community centers on the perimeter publishing stack (WAF / Reverse Proxy) that publishes the Customer Company 40 portal (portal.kf40.ch) and the network/firewall estate that supports user connectivity (including Teams media). A high‑priority support ticket (TI-1594408) documented an external 502 Bad Gateway outage for portal.kf40.ch while internal access remained functional. Troubleshooting showed the backend application servers and load balancer were healthy, TLS certificates were validated and not the root cause, and the outage correlated to a weekend security patch/change that reloaded a Default-Strict inspection/profile and removed a custom header rewrite rule needed to present X-Forwarded-Proto: https to the backend. Restoring the Portal-App-Template / custom header rewrite configuration per the Secure Web Publishing blueprint resolved external access. Separately, a Zurich site firewall hardening change restricted outbound UDP to ports 3478–3481 only, blocking the dynamic RTP port range and causing one‑way/no audio in Teams; a temporary port opening restored media flows and confirmed the root cause. The incident and artifacts highlight technical, process, and governance gaps around post‑patch template/profile validation, change control for perimeter devices, and runbooks for header/HTTP profile preservation during TLS inspection/termination. ## Root cause of portal 502: WAF TLS inspection plus missing/custom header rewrite rule (X-Forwarded-Proto) prevented the backend from seeing HTTPS. External requests to portal.kf40.ch returned 502 Bad Gateway while internal access remained functional; investigation showed the reverse proxy/WAF performs TLS termination/inspection and is expected to forward X-Forwarded-Proto: https to the backend, but that header was not present when inspection was active, causing the backend to misinterpret the request and return errors. This causal chain is documented in the incident artifacts linking the REVERSE PROXY / WAF to the APPLICATION SERVER and noting the missing CUSTOM HEADER REWRITE RULE and absent X-FORWARDED-PROTO header as drivers for the 502s [Data: Entities (302,312,319); Relationships (492,499,496)]. Restoring the header rewrite behavior per the Portal-App-Template/blueprint returned external functionality, confirming the header rewrite absence (not the backend or certificate validity) was the functional trigger for failures [Data: Entities (311,300); Relationships (568,499)]. ## Security patch/template reload replaced Portal-App-Template with Default-Strict, causing template/profile drift and header removal. A weekend security patch caused the reverse proxy/WAF to reload a default inspection/profile (Default-Strict) which replaced the custom PORTAL-APP-TEMPLATE and its documented HTTP/profile/header‑rewrite rules; the activated Default-Strict profile altered header-handling and removed or failed to preserve X-Forwarded headers, reproducing the 502 when TLS inspection was enabled [Data: Entities (310,311); Relationships (497,568,548)]. This shows configuration/template drift introduced by patching—where a device reset to a default profile overwrote application‑specific templates—rather than an application or certificate error, creating a clear process risk for perimeter devices that host per-application templates [Data: Relationships (497,562)]. ## Backend and load‑balancing layer were healthy—issue isolated to publishing/inspection layer. Load balancer health checks and internal tests marked the backend application server as healthy and reachable, indicating the backend process was not the primary failure point; monitoring and health checks were used to validate stability after the fix before ticket closure [Data: Entities (305,318,308); Relationships (504,505,513)]. These diagnostics materially constrained the fault domain to the perimeter (WAF/reverse proxy) and saved time by directing remediation to template/profile/header handling rather than application restarts alone [Data: Relationships (492,563)]. ## TLS certificate change was investigated and ruled out as the root cause. The customer reported a recently installed TLS certificate and helpdesk validated the certificate CN, SAN and chain; while the certificate change was temporally proximate to the outage, diagnostics showed certificate validity/chain issues were not the cause of the 502s—the publishing layer header behavior was [Data: Entities (306); Relationships (486)]. The certificate service and blueprint decision tree were consulted as part of the normal checklist, which helped rule TLS problems out and focus on WAF behavior [Data: Relationships (552,508)]. ## Teams media outage at Zurich caused by outbound firewall restriction blocking dynamic RTP ports. A separate but contemporaneous operational change at the Zurich site implemented a security-hardening outbound rule that permitted only UDP 3478–3481, blocking the dynamic RTP port range required for Teams media when Media Bypass is used. This change produced no audio for external calls; temporarily opening the wider UDP/RTP range restored audio immediately, confirming the firewall rule as the root cause [Data: Entities (303,442,443); Relationships (739,741,742,733)]. Firewall logs and change records tied the timing of the outbound restriction to the onset of audio issues, showing this was a local change management failure with direct impact to voice/media availability [Data: Relationships (738,739)]. ## Blueprint and operational docs exist but were not enforced by the post‑patch validation process. An authoritative Secure Web Publishing blueprint and versioned guidance (BLUEPRINT: SECURE WEB PUBLISHING – KF40 – VERSION 3.2 / WEB PUBLISHING BLUEPRINT) defines required behaviors: TLS termination at the WAF, required Portal-App-Template, and documented header rewrite rules and validation after patches. The team retrieved the blueprint from DMS to align remediation, indicating awareness of the standard; however, the blueprint's requirement to validate WAF profiles and preserve custom rewrite rules was not enforced automatically during the patch that reset profiles, producing configuration drift [Data: Entities (307,339,356); Relationships (508,512,562)]. This gap between blueprint and operational enforcement—where a profile reload caused a prohibited Default-Strict to be applied despite the blueprint forbidding it—exposes the environment to repeat regressions unless validation and change controls are hardened [Data: Relationships (548,497)]. ## Operational ownership, escalation path, and remediation were used correctly but could be formalized into runbooks and automated checks. The high-priority ticket TI-1594408 was owned initially by Employee 29 (helpdesk) who performed initial checks and escalated to BU Network & Security; BU Network & Security then took ownership and applied WAF/reverse proxy configuration changes to restore service, with monitoring confirming 24 hours stability before closure—showing correct functional escalation and remediation occurred [Data: Entities (295,298,299); Relationships (479,487,476)]. The artifacts show manual steps and handoffs (tests, template restores, monitoring) that resolved the incident, but the manual nature of these actions recommends formalizing a post‑patch WAF validation runbook, automated configuration drift detection, and explicit pre/post patch checklists to reduce mean‑time‑to‑repair and recurrence risk [Data: Relationships (479,513)].

Subsequently encrypted with a Sensitivity Label The encryption defined a different permission group than the SharePoint ACL. Token caching caused inconsistent behavior. 25.03.25 11:40 – Actions • Sensitivity Label removed • Folder reassigned with correct group reference • Label reapplied with correct group integration • “Compliance_Core” and “COMPLIANCE_TEAM” structurally aligned • Tests performed with multiple user accounts • Tokens intentionally invalidated 26.03.25 – Monitoring Multiple users tested Access consistently possible No further error messages 28.03.25 15:42 – Customer feedback Audit team confirms stable access for all employees. These relationships indicate tight coupling between application, identity federation, certificate lifecycle, and an integration service account—areas that drove the troubleshooting and remediation activity captured in the tickets and notes [Data: Entities (252, 235, 234, 254, 253); Relationships (405, 409, 410, 366, 369, 353)]. ## CE Train server depends on ADFS federation and was the primary failure locus The CE Train server (the affected application/server environment) is explicitly tied to ADFS as its identity/federation provider and was the focus of remediation and federation updates during the incident response. This dependency was acted on directly: federation data was updated on ADFS and ADFS configuration and certificates were checked/adjusted to address authentication failures affecting the CE Train server [Data: Entities (252), Relationships (405, 409)]. Because the CE Train server sits inside the broader TRAIN environment and is the component for which login/connectivity failures were reproduced across environments and machines, the server–ADFS trust relationship is a single point where misconfiguration, stale metadata, or certificate issues can produce wide-reaching authentication failures across integrations that rely on the TRAIN service [Data: Entities (252, 251), Relationships (411, 409)]. ## Certificates and federation metadata rotation were executed and are directly implicated Notes and ticket actions record removal of old certificates and updates to federation metadata as part of remediation on 02.12.24—these changes are recorded as intended to restore correct authentication flows for the CE Train server. The removal of old certificates and federation metadata updates are documented as direct relationships to the CE Train server and were among the primary configuration actions taken during the troubleshooting process [Data: Entities (253, 254, 252), Relationships (410, 409)]. This shows certificate and metadata lifecycle issues were a likely root or contributing cause; either expired/rotated certificates or mismatched federation metadata can break token issuance or claims handling in ADFS, which aligns with the observed JWT/MFA-related authentication symptoms described in ticket notes [Data: Entities (251), Relationships (405)]. ## A domain service account (KF11\\001-SVC-AXAVAOBT-TRA) is a suspected credential/configuration focal point Portal notes and relationship entries explicitly identify KF11\\001-SVC-AXAVAOBT-TRA as the service account currently configured for the integration with the TRAIN CE server, and investigators queried whether its password or credentials had changed. The service account is therefore a primary candidate for credential or configuration-related causes of the authentication failures [Data: Entities (234), Relationships (366)]. Because service accounts are commonly used by integration processes and are sensitive to password changes, expiration, or mis-scoped permissions, the documented attention on KF11\\001-SVC-AXAVAOBT-TRA indicates that investigating its credential state, account locks, and effective permissions should be a top operational activity during remediation and in future hardening steps [Data: Entities (234), Relationships (366)]. ## Ticket ownership, escalation path and cross-team involvement are documented but fragmented The support ticket timeline shows a multi-person workflow: the ticket was opened (Employee 16), assigned to Employee 14, transferred from Admin to Employee 24, and later handled by a System Engineer who performed repair work; external vendor Avanade and customer/tester Ms. X (absent until mid‑April) also participated in calls and validation cycles. This sequence demonstrates multiple handoffs and both internal and external stakeholders engaged during the incident [Data: Entities (251, 228, 231, 247, 7, 10, 13), Relationships (391, 353, 361, 19, 11, 12, 17)]. While the workflow shows engagement, it also highlights potential gaps: Ms. X’s unavailability for testing delayed validation activities, and ownership transfers (Admin → Employee 24 → System Engineer) could have introduced friction or knowledge loss. Clear escalation and acceptance criteria tied to the ticket (who validates repairs, when to escalate to Microsoft support, and who owns post-change verification) are not explicitly codified in the relationship notes and should be formalized to reduce incident MTTR [Data: Entities (251, 228, 13), Relationships (353, 361)]. ## Incident was reproducible across environments and prompted repair installation on 02.12.24 Ticket and portal notes state the error was reproduced across all environments, users and machines, and a repair installation on the CE Train server was recorded on 02.12.24. The System Engineer role is attributed with repair work and configuration checks/updates on the CE Train server and ADFS on that date, indicating that a system-level remediation step was performed as part of the incident response [Data: Entities (251, 252, 7), Relationships (367, 405)]. The reproducibility across environments increases confidence this was a configuration/identity issue (rather than a single-client problem) and justifies the system-level remediation and federation changes that followed; however, reproducibility also raises the need for broader regression testing in non-production environments after changes are applied to avoid collateral impact [Data: Entities (251), Relationships (405)]. ## TRAIN-CE/FO integration, DevOps and FO teams were impacted by the MFA/authentication behavior The ticket subject and relationships explicitly reference the TRAIN-CE/FO integration and name DevOps and FO as affected stakeholders, indicating that the authentication/MFA failures disrupted integration flows consumed by these teams. The integration depends on ADFS-validated tokens and Toolbox MFA flows, which were part of the scope of the investigation [Data: Entities (250, 245, 246), Relationships (369, 388, 389)]. Operational impact on DevOps and FO implies business-process consequences beyond single-user logins (for example, CI/CD tasks, front-office workflows, or automated integrations), so restoring stable federation and MFA handling is necessary to resume normal cross-team operations [Data: Entities (250), Relationships (369)]. ## Network infrastructure was investigated and explicitly excluded as the root cause During troubleshooting the network layer was explicitly examined and investigators reported 'No irregularities were found in the network', after which the network infrastructure was excluded as the root cause of the incident. This focused the investigation on identity federation, certificates, service accounts and application configuration rather than transport-layer issues [Data: Entities (22), Relationships (13)]. Documenting this exclusion is useful operationally because it prevents redundant network-level remediation and helps prioritize identity and certificate workstreams during follow-up change planning and monitoring adjustments [Data: Entities (22), Relationships (13)]. ## Recommendations — prioritized action items for remediation, governance and runbooks Based on the documented relationships and remediation steps, prioritized actions are: (1) validate the KF11\\001-SVC-AXAVAOBT-TRA service account credential state, lockout history and expiration policy; (2) verify ADFS federation metadata and automate monitoring/alerts for metadata/certificate mismatches; (3) formalize certificate lifecycle management for the TRAIN↔ADFS trust (document owners, rotation schedule, pre-expiry testing); (4) produce a compact escalation/runbook that maps ticket ownership transitions (Admin → Employee 24 → Employee 14 → System Engineer → Microsoft support) and defines acceptance testing responsibilities (including contingency when Ms. X is unavailable); (5) add post-change regression tests for the TRAIN-CE/FO integration to detect token/claims/MFA regressions before closing tickets [Data: Entities (234, 253, 254, 251, 228, 7), Relationships (366, 410, 409, 353, 361, 405)]. Each recommendation directly corresponds to the observed evidence: the service account is explicitly noted as configured for the integration and was queried by investigators; certificates and federation metadata were changed during remediation; the ticket history shows multiple ownership transfers and reliance on specific individuals for validation; and the integration scope names DevOps and FO as impacted consumers—collectively supporting these prioritized governance and operational controls [Data: Entities (234, 253, 254, 251, 245, 246), Relationships (366, 410, 409, 353, 369, 388)].

Closure description: Parameters adjusted on ADFS as in the TEST environment. 18.12.24 00:34 Re: TRAIN-CE/FO integration / MFA Dear Employee 7, During the joint analysis of the issue, we identified that two parameters were not correctly set during the ADFS client registration. In the new client application used specifically for the MFA request of the Toolbox on the Train system, the following parameters were set to true: EnableJWT RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders EnableJWT activates the issuance of JSON Web Tokens in the authentication process. If enabled, ADFS issues JWTs instead of other token formats such as SAML. These tokens are evaluated by the target application. If the target system does not support JWTs or cannot validate their signature, authentication fails. RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders forwards MFA requirements to configured claims providers. This delegates MFA execution to external providers and increases complexity in authentication flow, logging, and auditing. The combination of these parameters can lead to login failures if the target system is not designed to handle JWTs or external MFA claims. We have documented the configuration to avoid future issues. From our perspective, no further problems are expected after adjusting the parameters. If MFA works stably and the Train environment is reachable again, the login topic can be considered resolved.
